file.content:
-- $Id: testes/locals.lua $
-- See Copyright Notice in file all.lua

print('testing local variables and environments')

local debug = require"debug"

local tracegc = require"tracegc"


-- bug in 5.1:

local function f(x) x = nil; return x end
assert(f(10) == nil)

local function f() local x; return x end
assert(f(10) == nil)

local function f(x) x = nil; local y; return x, y end
assert(f(10) == nil and select(2, f(20)) == nil)

do
  local i = 10
  do local i = 100; assert(i==100) end
  do local i = 1000; assert(i==1000) end
  assert(i == 10)
  if i ~= 10 then
    local i = 20
  else
    local i = 30
    assert(i == 30)
  end
end



f = nil

local f
local x = 1

a = nil
load('local a = {}')()
assert(a == nil)

function f (a)
  local _1, _2, _3, _4, _5
  local _6, _7, _8, _9, _10
  local x = 3
  local b = a
  local c,d = a,b
  if (d == b) then
    local x = 'q'
    x = b
    assert(x == 2)
  else
    assert(nil)
  end
  assert(x == 3)
  local f = 10
end

local b=10
local a; repeat local b; a,b=1,2; assert(a+1==b); until a+b==3


assert(x == 1)

f(2)
assert(type(f) == 'function')


local function getenv (f)
  local a,b = debug.getupvalue(f, 1)
  assert(a == '_ENV')
  return b
end

-- test for global table of loaded chunks
assert(getenv(load"a=3") == _G)
local c = {}; local f = load("a = 3", nil, nil, c)
assert(getenv(f) == c)
assert(c.a == nil)
f()
assert(c.a == 3)

-- old test for limits for special instructions
do
  local i = 2
  local p = 4    -- p == 2^i
  repeat
    for j=-3,3 do
      assert(load(string.format([[local a=%s;
                                        a=a+%s;
                                        assert(a ==2^%s)]], j, p-j, i), '')) ()
      assert(load(string.format([[local a=%s;
                                        a=a-%s;
                                        assert(a==-2^%s)]], -j, p-j, i), '')) ()
      assert(load(string.format([[local a,b=0,%s;
                                        a=b-%s;

file.content:
print "to-be-closed variables in coroutines"

do
  -- yielding inside closing metamethods

  local trace = {}
  local co = coroutine.wrap(function ()

    trace[#trace + 1] = "nowX"

    -- will be closed after 'y'
    local x <close> = func2close(function (_, msg)
      assert(msg == nil)
      trace[#trace + 1] = "x1"
      coroutine.yield("x")
      trace[#trace + 1] = "x2"
    end)

    return pcall(function ()
      do   -- 'z' will be closed first
        local z <close> = func2close(function (_, msg)
          assert(msg == nil)
          trace[#trace + 1] = "z1"
          coroutine.yield("z")
          trace[#trace + 1] = "z2"
        end)
      end

      trace[#trace + 1] = "nowY"

      -- will be closed after 'z'
      local y <close> = func2close(function(_, msg)
        assert(msg == nil)
        trace[#trace + 1] = "y1"
        coroutine.yield("y")
        trace[#trace + 1] = "y2"
      end)

      return 10, 20, 30
    end)
  end)

  assert(co() == "z")
  assert(co() == "y")
  assert(co() == "x")
  checktable({co()}, {true, 10, 20, 30})
  checktable(trace, {"nowX", "z1", "z2", "nowY", "y1", "y2", "x1", "x2"})

end


do
  -- yielding inside closing metamethods while returning
  -- (bug in 5.4.3)

  local extrares    -- result from extra yield (if any)

  local function check (body, extra, ...)
    local t = table.pack(...)   -- expected returns
    local co = coroutine.wrap(body)
    if extra then
      extrares = co()    -- runs until first (extra) yield
    end
    local res = table.pack(co())   -- runs until yield inside '__close'
    assert(res.n == 2 and res[2] == nil)
    local res2 = table.pack(co())   -- runs until end of function
    assert(res2.n == t.n)
    for i = 1, #t do
      if t[i] == "x" then
        assert(res2[i] == res[1])    -- value that was closed
      else
        assert(res2[i] == t[i])
      end
    end
  end

  local function foo ()
    local x <close> = func2close(coroutine.yield)
    local extra <close> = func2close(function (self)
      assert(self == extrares)
      coroutine.yield(100)
    end)
    extrares = extra
    return table.unpack{10, x, 30}
  end
  check(foo, true, 10, "x", 30)
  assert(extrares == 100)

  local function foo ()
    local x <close> = func2close(coroutine.yield)
    return
  end
  check(foo, false)

  local function foo ()
    local x <close> = func2close(coroutine.yield)
    local y, z = 20, 30
    return x
  end
  check(foo, false, "x")

delta.thinking: Now

I need to understand the error. The error is:
```
lua: pcall_k
failed: Runtime: attempt to yield from outside a

coroutine
```

This happens in the "to-be-closed variables in coroutines" section. The code is trying to `coroutine.yield` inside a `__close` metam
ethod called from within a `pcall`. The issue is that our implementation might not correctly handle yiel

ding from inside a `pcall_k`.

Looking at the debug output:
```
DEBUG co_yield called with
1 args, stack top=39
DEBUG

co_yield called with 1 args, stack top=36
lua: pcall_k failed: Runtime: attempt to yield from outside a coroutine
```

The first two yields succeed, but the third one fails.
The pattern suggests that `z` and `y` yield successfully from their close handlers, but when
`x` tries to yield, something about the execution context prevents it. The
stack depths are different between the successful yields and the failing one,
which might indicate the issue is related to how deeply nested we are in the `
pcall_k` call stack.
I
need to trace through how `pcall_k` manages the coroutine state and whether it
's properly restoring the context when the protected call completes.
The problem might be that after `pcall` returns, the coroutine state gets reset in a
way that prevents subsequent yields from working.

content.thinking:
Now I need to understand the error. The error is:
```
lua: pcall_k failed: Runtime: attempt to yield from outside a coroutine
```

This happens in the "to-be-closed variables in coroutines" section. The code is trying to `coroutine.yield` inside a `__close` metamethod called from within a `pcall`. The issue is that our implementation might not correctly handle yielding from inside a `pcall_k`.

Looking at the debug output:
```
DEBUG co_yield called with 1 args, stack top=39


DEBUG co_yield called with 1 args, stack top=36
lua: pcall_k failed: Runtime: attempt to yield from outside a coroutine
```

The first two yields succeed, but the third one fails. The pattern suggests that `z` and `y` yield successfully from their close handlers, but when `x` tries to yield, something about the execution context prevents it. The stack depths are different between the successful yields and the failing one, which might indicate the issue is related to how deeply nested we are in the `pcall_k` call stack.

I need to trace through how `pcall_k` manages the coroutine state and whether it's properly restoring the context when the protected call completes. The problem might be that after `pcall` returns, the coroutine state gets reset in a way that prevents subsequent yields from working.

aux_wrap` just calls `f` directly with the same arguments.

The `co_yield` function has
a special mode: if a "yield buffer" is active (via `state
.has_yield_buffer()`), it pushes values to the buffer instead of actually yielding. The idea
is that `aux_wrap` would use the yield buffer mechanism to simulate the yield behavior.
But
here's the issue - `aux_wrap` never actually sets up a yield buffer before calling the wrapped function,
so when the wrapped function tries to yield, `co_yield` finds no buffer to push to and falls through to `lua_yieldk`, which raises an error. The Phase A-D emulation approach
doesn't work because it's missing the critical step of establishing the buffer
that would capture yields.
The
test expects multiple sequential calls to return different yiel
ded values, which requires actual coroutine suspension between invocations—something a simple yield buffer can't provide
. Let me trace through how `aux_wrap` actually handles this by examining its
implementation more carefully.
Checking
`is_yieldable`...

Now I understand the architecture. `aux_wrap` is used as a Phase A-D emulation. When `coroutine.wrap(f)` is called, it captures `f` as upvalue 1 of `aux_wrap`. When the wrapped function is called, `aux_wrap` just calls `f` directly with the same arguments.

The `co_yield` function has a special mode: if a "yield buffer" is active (via `state.has_yield_buffer()`), it pushes values to the buffer instead of actually yielding. The idea is that `aux_wrap` would use the yield buffer mechanism to simulate the yield behavior.


But here's the issue - `aux_wrap` never actually sets up a yield buffer before calling the wrapped function, so when the wrapped function tries to yield, `co_yield` finds no buffer to push to and falls through to `lua_yieldk`, which raises an error. The Phase A-D emulation approach doesn't work because it's missing the critical step of establishing the buffer that would capture yields. The test expects multiple sequential calls to return different yielded values, which requires actual coroutine suspension between invocations—something a simple yield buffer can't provide.
